questions.subject: Physics

questions.text: Two fog droplets have radii 2:3, their terminal velocities are
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The formula for terminal velocity is vt= 2gr2 ρ/9ηSince the terminal velocity vt is proportional to the square of the radius ( r2 ) the ratio of their terminal velocities will be the square of the ratio of their radii. Given the ratio of radii as 2:3, the square of this ratio is ( 2 / 3 )2 = 4 / 9. Therefore, the ratio of their terminal velocities will be 4:9

questions.text: Venturi meter is used to measure
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A Venturi meter is used to measure the fluid speed or flow velocity within a pipe. It utilizes the principle of the Venturi effect to determine the velocity of the fluid passing through it by measuring the pressure difference between the narrow and wider sections of the pipe.

questions.text: The blood flow is_ flow at systolic pressure

questions.text: The effect of the decrease in pressure with the increase in speed of the fluid in horizontal tube gives that:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This phenomenon is a consequence of Bernoulli's principle, which states that as the speed of a fluid increases, its pressure decreases. In a horizontal tube, when the fluid (such as air or a liquid) speeds up, the kinetic energy of the fluid increases, causing a decrease in pressure according to Bernoulli's principle.

questions.text: With the increase of temperature viscosity.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Viscosity is a measure of a fluid's resistance to flow. In general, as the temperature of a substance increases, its molecular motion and kinetic energy also increase. In liquids, higher temperatures lead to weaker intermolecular forces, allowing molecules to move more freely and reducing the resistance to flow. As a result, the viscosity of most liquids tends to decrease with an increase in temperature. This behavior is more pronounced in liquids with stronger intermolecular forces at lower temperatures.

questions.explanation: Viscosity is a measure of a fluid's resistance to flow. In general, as the temperature of a substance increases, its molecular motion and kinetic energy also increase. In liquids, higher temperatures lead to weaker intermolecular forces, allowing molecules to move more freely and reducing the resistance to flow. As a result, the viscosity of most liquids tends to decrease with an increase in temperature. This behavior is more pronounced in liquids with stronger intermolecular forces at lower temperatures.

questions.text: The acceleration of a particle moving with terminal velocity through a fluid is Options:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Positive - Acceleration is not positive because the particle has reached a constant velocity.
Negative - Acceleration is not negative because the particle has reached a constant velocity.

When a particle reaches terminal velocity in a fluid, it means that the gravitational force pulling it down is balanced by the drag force acting upwards. At terminal velocity, the net force on the particle is zero, so its acceleration is zero as well.
Cannot produce - This option is incorrect as terminal velocity has already been achieved, so the acceleration is zero.

questions.explanation: When a particle reaches terminal velocity in a fluid, it means that the gravitational force pulling it down is balanced by the drag force acting upwards. At terminal velocity, the net force on the particle is zero, so its acceleration is zero as well.

In liquids, higher temperatures lead to weaker intermolecular forces, allowing molecules to move more freely and reducing the resistance to flow. As a result, the viscosity of most liquids tends to decrease with an increase in temperature. This behavior is more pronounced in liquids with stronger intermolecular forces at lower temperatures.
Due to the explanation above, it is incorrect.

questions.text: The SI unit of the coefficient of viscosity is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This unit doesn't represent the coefficient of viscosity. It lacks the time unit.

The SI unit of the coefficient of viscosity, also known as dynamic viscosity, is expressed in kg m^-1s^-1. This unit represents the force per unit area required to maintain a unit velocity gradient between two parallel layers of fluid separated by a unit distance.
This unit is incorrect as it doesn't have the inverse length (m^-1) required for viscosity.

questions.text: A liquid moves through a horizontal pipe. The pressure is
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: lower where the diameter is large
options.explanation: In an incompressible fluid (assuming the liquid is incompressible) flowing through a horizontal pipe, Bernoulli's principle applies. This principle states that for an incompressible fluid in steady flow, the sum of the pressure head, kinetic energy head, and potential energy head remains constant throughout the flow path.Pressure head: This refers to the pressure (P) divided by the density (ρ) of the fluid.Kinetic energy head: This is the kinetic energy (KE) of the fluid per unit weight, calculated as KE / (ρg), where g is the acceleration due to gravity.Potential energy head: This is the potential energy (PE) of the fluid per unit weight, calculated as PE / (ρg), where PE is typically the height of the fluid above a reference point.In a horizontal pipe, the potential energy head remains constant (since there's no change in height). Therefore, for constant flow rate (meaning the same amount of liquid passes through any point in the pipe per unit time), the following relationship holds:P1/ρ + KE1/(ρg) = P2/ρ + KE2/(ρg)where:P1 and P2 are the pressures at two different points in the pipeKE1 and KE2 are the kinetic energies at the corresponding pointsSince the pipe is horizontal (no change in height), the potential energy heads (PE/ρg) are equal at all points. This leaves us with:P1 + KE1/ρ = P2 + KE2/ρIf the diameter is larger at a specific point (point 2), the flow velocity (v) will be lower at that point compared to a narrower diameter section (point 1) due to the principle of continuity (mass flow rate remains constant). This relationship can be expressed as:A1 * v1 = A2 * v2where:A1 and A2 are the cross-sectional areas at points 1 and 2, respectivelySince the area (A) is proportional to the square of the radius (r), doubling the radius (doubling the diameter) results in a four-fold increase in the area (A). To maintain the same mass flow rate, the velocity (v) at the wider section (point 2) must decrease by a factor of 1/4 compared to the narrower section (point 1).Combining these relationships:Lower v2 leads to a lower kinetic energy head (KE2) at the wider section compared to the narrower section.Since the sum of pressure and kinetic energy head needs to be constant, the pressure (P2) at the wider section must be lower to compensate for the lower kinetic energy head.Therefore, the pressure is lower where the diameter is large in an incompressible fluid flowing through a horizontal pipe based on Bernoulli's principle and the principle of continuity.

options.text: higher where the diameter is large
options.explanation: This is the opposite of the correct answer. As explained above, the pressure is lower in wider sections due to the relationship between pressure, velocity, and flow rate.

options.text: same throughout the pipe but less than atmospheric pressure
options.explanation: While the pressure might be less than atmospheric in specific scenarios (e.g., due to external factors like pumps), it wouldn't be the same throughout the pipe if the diameter varies as explained in the correct answer.

options.text: same throughout the pipe but more than atmospheric pressure
options.explanation: Similar to (c), the pressure wouldn't be the same throughout the pipe due to varying diameters. Additionally, unless external forces are applied, the pressure wouldn't necessarily be higher than atmospheric pressure throughout the horizontal pipe.

questions.explanation: In an incompressible fluid (assuming the liquid is incompressible) flowing through a horizontal pipe, Bernoulli's principle applies. This principle states that for an incompressible fluid in steady flow, the sum of the pressure head, kinetic energy head, and potential energy head remains constant throughout the flow path.Pressure head: This refers to the pressure (P) divided by the density (ρ) of the fluid.Kinetic energy head: This is the kinetic energy (KE) of the fluid per unit weight, calculated as KE / (ρg), where g is the acceleration due to gravity.Potential energy head: This is the potential energy (PE) of the fluid per unit weight, calculated as PE / (ρg), where PE is typically the height of the fluid above a reference point.In a horizontal pipe, the potential energy head remains constant (since there's no change in height). Therefore, for constant flow rate (meaning the same amount of liquid passes through any point in the pipe per unit time), the following relationship holds:P1/ρ + KE1/(ρg) = P2/ρ + KE2/(ρg)where:P1 and P2 are the pressures at two different points in the pipeKE1 and KE2 are the kinetic energies at the corresponding pointsSince the pipe is horizontal (no change in height), the potential energy heads (PE/ρg) are equal at all points. This leaves us with:P1 + KE1/ρ = P2 + KE2/ρIf the diameter is larger at a specific point (point 2), the flow velocity (v) will be lower at that point compared to a narrower diameter section (point 1) due to the principle of continuity (mass flow rate remains constant). This relationship can be expressed as:A1 * v1 = A2 * v2where:A1 and A2 are the cross-sectional areas at points 1 and 2, respectivelySince the area (A) is proportional to the square of the radius (r), doubling the radius (doubling the diameter) results in a four-fold increase in the area (A). To maintain the same mass flow rate, the velocity (v) at the wider section (point 2) must decrease by a factor of 1/4 compared to the narrower section (point 1).Combining these relationships:Lower v2 leads to a lower kinetic energy head (KE2) at the wider section compared to the narrower section.Since the sum of pressure and kinetic energy head needs to be constant, the pressure (P2) at the wider section must be lower to compensate for the lower kinetic energy head.Therefore, the pressure is lower where the diameter is large in an incompressible fluid flowing through a horizontal pipe based on Bernoulli's principle and the principle of continuity.

questions.text: Bernoulli's equation is based upon the conservation law of
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Bernoulli's equation is not based on the conservation of mass.

Bernoulli's equation is based on the conservation of energy along a streamline in a flowing fluid. It states that the total mechanical energy per unit mass of a fluid is constant along a streamline. This energy includes kinetic energy, potential energy, and internal energy per unit mass.
Bernoulli's equation does not directly involve the conservation of momentum.
While Bernoulli's equation involves the conservation of energy, it does not directly incorporate the conservation of mass or momentum.

questions.explanation: Bernoulli's equation is based on the conservation of energy along a streamline in a flowing fluid. It states that the total mechanical energy per unit mass of a fluid is constant along a streamline. This energy includes kinetic energy, potential energy, and internal energy per unit mass.

The flow rate wouldn't remain the same if the radius changes. Changing the tube's diameter affects the cross-sectional area through which the liquid flows, impacting the flow rate.

questions.explanation: According to the equation of continuity, the rate of flow of a liquid is inversely proportional to the square of the radius of the tube. Therefore, if the radius is doubled, the rate of flow increases by a factor of 1/4.

questions.text: A pipe having an internal diameter 'D' to another pipe of same size, water flow into the second pipe through 'n' holes, each of diameter 'd'. if the water in the first pipe has speed 'V' the speed of water leaving the second pipe is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option flips the exponent of D and d, resulting in an incorrect relationship
This option multiplies D² instead of dividing it, leading to an incorrect relationship

Continuity Principle:The principle of continuity states that in an incompressible fluid, the mass flow rate remains constant throughout a stream tube (a tube whose walls are formed by streamlines).Mass Flow Rate:Mass flow rate (ṁ) is the mass of fluid passing a point per unit time. It can be calculated using the following equation:ṁ = ρAVwhere:ρ (rho) is the fluid density.A is the cross-sectional area of the flow channel.V is the fluid velocity.Applying the Continuity Principle:In this problem, we have two pipes:First pipe: Diameter = D, Velocity = V, Area (A₁) = πD²/4Second pipe: Diameter = D (same as first pipe), n holes each with diameter d, Area of each hole (A₂) = πd²/4Since the water is incompressible and there's no external source or sink within the system, the mass flow rate entering the second pipe (through the n holes) must be equal to the mass flow rate leaving the first pipe.Calculating the Mass Flow Rates:Mass flow rate in the first pipe (ṁ₁):ṁ₁ = ρ * (πD²/4) * VMass flow rate through each hole in the second pipe (ṁ₂):ṁ₂ = ρ * (πd²/4) * v₂ (where v₂ is the unknown velocity leaving the second pipe)Total mass flow rate in the second pipe (ṁ₃):ṁ₃ = n * ṁ₂ = n * ρ * (πd²/4) * v₂Equating Mass Flow Rates:According to the continuity principle, ṁ₁ = ṁ₃:ρ * (πD²/4) * V = n * ρ * (πd²/4) * v₂Solving for v₂ (velocity leaving the second pipe):Dividing both sides by ρ and rearranging the equation:v₂ = (D²/nd²) * VTherefore, the speed of water leaving the second pipe (v₂) is nd² v / D², which corresponds to option c. nd² v/D²
This option squares d incorrectly and divides by nd², leading to an incorrect relationship

questions.explanation: Continuity Principle:The principle of continuity states that in an incompressible fluid, the mass flow rate remains constant throughout a stream tube (a tube whose walls are formed by streamlines).Mass Flow Rate:Mass flow rate (ṁ) is the mass of fluid passing a point per unit time. It can be calculated using the following equation:ṁ = ρAVwhere:ρ (rho) is the fluid density.A is the cross-sectional area of the flow channel.V is the fluid velocity.Applying the Continuity Principle:In this problem, we have two pipes:First pipe: Diameter = D, Velocity = V, Area (A₁) = πD²/4Second pipe: Diameter = D (same as first pipe), n holes each with diameter d, Area of each hole (A₂) = πd²/4Since the water is incompressible and there's no external source or sink within the system, the mass flow rate entering the second pipe (through the n holes) must be equal to the mass flow rate leaving the first pipe.Calculating the Mass Flow Rates:Mass flow rate in the first pipe (ṁ₁):ṁ₁ = ρ * (πD²/4) * VMass flow rate through each hole in the second pipe (ṁ₂):ṁ₂ = ρ * (πd²/4) * v₂ (where v₂ is the unknown velocity leaving the second pipe)Total mass flow rate in the second pipe (ṁ₃):ṁ₃ = n * ṁ₂ = n * ρ * (πd²/4) * v₂Equating Mass Flow Rates:According to the continuity principle, ṁ₁ = ṁ₃:ρ * (πD²/4) * V = n * ρ * (πd²/4) * v₂Solving for v₂ (velocity leaving the second pipe):Dividing both sides by ρ and rearranging the equation:v₂ = (D²/nd²) * VTherefore, the speed of water leaving the second pipe (v₂) is nd² v / D², which corresponds to option c. nd² v/D²

questions.text: The maximum dragging force on a sphere of weight 9.8 N is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The maximum dragging force on a sphere moving through a fluid is equal to its weight, which is 9.8 N in this case. Drag force equals the gravitational force acting on the object when it is moving at terminal velocity. Thus, the maximum dragging force is equal to the weight of the sphere.
This is half of the weight of the sphere, not the maximum dragging force.

Drag force equals the gravitational force acting on the object when it is moving at terminal velocity. Thus, the maximum dragging force is equal to the weight of the sphere.

questions.text: Above critical velocity, the fluid flow is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Laminar flow is smooth and orderly, occurring at low flow rates and below the critical velocity.
The term "regular" isn't typically used to describe fluid flow regimes; instead, laminar or turbulent flow is discussed
Streamline flow is a characteristic of laminar flow, not turbulent flow. Turbulent flow is the opposite of streamline flow.

Above the critical velocity, the fluid flow becomes turbulent. Turbulent flow is characterized by chaotic and irregular movement of fluid particles. It occurs at high flow rates and is associated with eddies, swirls, and mixing within the fluid.

questions.explanation: Above the critical velocity, the fluid flow becomes turbulent. Turbulent flow is characterized by chaotic and irregular movement of fluid particles. It occurs at high flow rates and is associated with eddies, swirls, and mixing within the fluid.

questions.text: The instrument used to measure blood pressure is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A barometer is used to measure atmospheric pressure, not blood pressure.

A sphygmomanometer is the instrument used to measure blood pressure. It consists of an inflatable cuff that is wrapped around the upper arm and a pressure gauge (manometer) for measuring blood pressure.
A venturi meter is used to measure the flow rate of fluids in pipes.
A stethoscope is used for auscultation, primarily to listen to sounds produced by the heart, lungs, or other internal organs, but it is not used to measure blood pressure directly.

questions.text: A capillary tube of radius r is immersed in water and water rises to a height of 'h'. Mass of water in the capillary tube is5 x 10 -3kg. The same capillary tube in now immersed in a liquid whose surface tension is √2 times the surface tension of water. The angle of contact between the capillary tube and this is liquid is 45º. The mass of liquid which rises into the capillary tube now is .(in kg)
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Relate mass of water to height and density:The mass of water (m_water) can be expressed as the product of its density (ρ_water), the cross-sectional area of the capillary tube (A), and the height of the water column (h):m_water = ρ_water * A * hSolve for the area (A):Rearrange the equation above to solve for the area:A = m_water / (ρ_water * h)Plug in the given values:A = (5 x 10^-3 kg) / (1000 kg/m³ * h)Relate pressure difference to surface tension and curvature:The Young-Laplace equation states:ΔP = 2 * γ / rwhere:ΔP is the pressure difference across the interface (positive due to surface tension pulling the liquid upwards)γ (gamma) is the surface tension of the liquidr is the radius of the capillary tubeRelate pressure difference to equilibrium:At equilibrium, the pressure difference due to surface tension balances the pressure difference due to the weight of the liquid column:ΔP = ρ_liquid * g * hwhere:ρ_liquid is the density of the second liquidg is the acceleration due to gravityCombine equations and solve for h':Substitute the expressions for ΔP from both equations 3 and 4:2 * γ / r = ρ_liquid * g * h'where h' is the height of the second liquid.Since the radius and angle of contact are the same for both liquids, and the contact angle is 45° (cos(45°) = √2/2), we can write:h' = (2 * γ * cos(45°)) / (ρ_liquid * g * r) = (γ * √2) / (ρ_liquid * g * r)Relate mass of the second liquid to its height and density:Similar to step 1, express the mass of the second liquid (m_liquid):m_liquid = ρ_liquid * A * h'Substitute and solve for m_liquid:Substitute the expression for A from step 2 and h' from step 5:m_liquid = ρ_liquid * (m_water / (ρ_water * h)) * [(γ * √2) / (ρ_liquid * g * r)]Since the radius, angle of contact, and g are constant, and the density of water (ρ_water) is assumed to be known, we can combine them into a constant factor (K):m_liquid = K * (γ * √2) / ρ_liquidCompare the mass of the second liquid:The mass of the second liquid (m_liquid) is proportional to the surface tension of the second liquid (γ) and inversely proportional to its density (ρ_liquid).Since the surface tension of the second liquid is √2 times that of water, and assuming the densities are comparable, the mass of the second liquid will be:m_liquid ≈ √2 * m_water (assuming similar densities)Therefore, the mass of the second liquid that rises in the capillary tube is approximately √2 times the mass of water, which corresponds to option c) 5 √2 x 10^-3 kg

questions.explanation: Relate mass of water to height and density:The mass of water (m_water) can be expressed as the product of its density (ρ_water), the cross-sectional area of the capillary tube (A), and the height of the water column (h):m_water = ρ_water * A * hSolve for the area (A):Rearrange the equation above to solve for the area:A = m_water / (ρ_water * h)Plug in the given values:A = (5 x 10^-3 kg) / (1000 kg/m³ * h)Relate pressure difference to surface tension and curvature:The Young-Laplace equation states:ΔP = 2 * γ / rwhere:ΔP is the pressure difference across the interface (positive due to surface tension pulling the liquid upwards)γ (gamma) is the surface tension of the liquidr is the radius of the capillary tubeRelate pressure difference to equilibrium:At equilibrium, the pressure difference due to surface tension balances the pressure difference due to the weight of the liquid column:ΔP = ρ_liquid * g * hwhere:ρ_liquid is the density of the second liquidg is the acceleration due to gravityCombine equations and solve for h':Substitute the expressions for ΔP from both equations 3 and 4:2 * γ / r = ρ_liquid * g * h'where h' is the height of the second liquid.Since the radius and angle of contact are the same for both liquids, and the contact angle is 45° (cos(45°) = √2/2), we can write:h' = (2 * γ * cos(45°)) / (ρ_liquid * g * r) = (γ * √2) / (ρ_liquid * g * r)Relate mass of the second liquid to its height and density:Similar to step 1, express the mass of the second liquid (m_liquid):m_liquid = ρ_liquid * A * h'Substitute and solve for m_liquid:Substitute the expression for A from step 2 and h' from step 5:m_liquid = ρ_liquid * (m_water / (ρ_water * h)) * [(γ * √2) / (ρ_liquid * g * r)]Since the radius, angle of contact, and g are constant, and the density of water (ρ_water) is assumed to be known, we can combine them into a constant factor (K):m_liquid = K * (γ * √2) / ρ_liquidCompare the mass of the second liquid:The mass of the second liquid (m_liquid) is proportional to the surface tension of the second liquid (γ) and inversely proportional to its density (ρ_liquid).Since the surface tension of the second liquid is √2 times that of water, and assuming the densities are comparable, the mass of the second liquid will be:m_liquid ≈ √2 * m_water (assuming similar densities)Therefore, the mass of the second liquid that rises in the capillary tube is approximately √2 times the mass of water, which corresponds to option c) 5 √2 x 10^-3 kg

As the velocity (v) of the fluid increases, the pressure (P) decreases to maintain a constant value on the left side of the equation. questions.text: How much water a pump of 2kw can raise in one minute to a height of 10 m, (Take g =10m/s2 )
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Due to the explanation above, it is incorrect.

The work done by the pump is given by the formula:Work=Power×TimeIn this case, the power of the pump is given as 2 kW, and we need to find the work done in lifting water to a height of 10 m in one minute.First, let's convert the power from kilowatts to watts (1 kW = 1000 W):Power=2 kW×1000 W/kW=2000WNow, we can use the formula for work done:Work=Power×TimeSince the time is given as one minute, we need to convert it to seconds (1 minute = 60 seconds):Time=1 minute×60 seconds/minute=60 Now, we can calculate the work done:Work=2000 W×60 s=120000 JoulesThe potential energy gained by lifting the water is given by:Potential Energy=Mass×g×HeightSince Potential Energy=Work Done, we can equate the two expressions:Mass×g×Height=120000JoulesNow, we can solve for the mass of water:Mass=120000 Joules/gxheightSubstitute the given values (g = 10 m/s², Height = 10 m):Mass=120000 Joules/10 m/s²×10 m=1200 kgFinally, convert the mass from kilograms to liters (1 kg of water = 1 liter):Volume (liters)=1200 liters
Due to the explanation above, it is incorrect.

questions.text: At terminal velocity, fluid friction is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Due to the explanation above, it is incorrect.
Due to the explanation above, it is incorrect.

Terminal velocity is the constant speed that a freely falling object eventually reaches when the resistance of the medium (such as air or water) through which it is falling prevents further acceleration. At terminal velocity, the forces are balanced, and the object experiences zero net force. This means that the fluid friction (air resistance) is equal to the force of gravity, and the object moves with a constant speed. Therefore, at terminal velocity, fluid friction is neither increasing nor decreasing; it is effectively zero in the sense that there is no further acceleration.
Due to the explanation above, it is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Terminal velocity is the constant speed that a freely falling object eventually reaches when the resistance of the medium (such as air or water) through which it is falling prevents further acceleration. At terminal velocity, the forces are balanced, and the object experiences zero net force. This means that the fluid friction (air resistance) is equal to the force of gravity, and the object moves with a constant speed. Therefore, at terminal velocity, fluid friction is neither increasing nor decreasing; it is effectively zero in the sense that there is no further acceleration.

questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The equation of continuity is based on the principle of mass conservation for fluids. It states that the mass flow rate of a fluid is constant within a pipe or a tube of varying cross-sectional area. The equation is A1​v1​=A2​v2​, where A is the cross-sectional area and v is the fluid velocity.

Bernoulli's theorem is based on the principle of conservation of energy for fluids. It states that in a streamline flow of a non-viscous, incompressible fluid, the total energy per unit mass (comprising kinetic energy, potential energy, and pressure energy) remains constant.
The given equation is associated with Torricelli's Law, which states the speed (v) of efflux of a fluid from an orifice under the influence of gravity alone.

Compressible fluids involve changes in density, and the behavior is more complex than the simple relationship shown in the provided equation. Equations for compressible fluids often involve considerations of compressibility and thermodynamic properties.

questions.text: With the increase of temperature, viscosity:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option suggests that viscosity increases with temperature. In most cases for liquids, this is not a common trend. As temperature rises, molecules tend to gain more kinetic energy, causing them to move more freely and reducing the viscosity.

The relationship between temperature and viscosity can vary depending on the substance. In general, for many liquids, as temperature increases, viscosity tends to decrease. This is because higher temperatures typically lead to greater molecular motion and reduced intermolecular forces, making the substance flow more easily.

In some cases, the viscosity of a substance may remain relatively constant over a certain temperature range. This typically occurs when the effects of temperature on different factors (such as molecular motion and intermolecular forces) counteract each other, resulting in a relatively stable viscosity.
This is an extreme option and is generally not a common behavior for most liquids. Doubling viscosity with an increase in temperature is not a typical trend for ordinary fluids.

questions.explanation: The relationship between temperature and viscosity can vary depending on the substance. In general, for many liquids, as temperature increases, viscosity tends to decrease. This is because higher temperatures typically lead to greater molecular motion and reduced intermolecular forces, making the substance flow more easily.

questions.text: In case of streamed lined flow of liquid the loss of energy is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option suggests that in streamlined flow, the loss of energy is maximum. However, this is not accurate. Streamlined or laminar flow is characterized by smooth, parallel layers of fluid moving in an orderly fashion, resulting in lower frictional losses compared to turbulent flow. Therefore, the loss of energy is minimized in streamlined flow.

In the case of streamlined (laminar) flow of liquid, the loss of energy is typically at a minimum. This is because streamlined flow is characterized by smooth and orderly movement of fluid particles in parallel layers, resulting in lower frictional losses compared to turbulent flow.

This option suggests that the loss of energy in streamlined flow is infinite. This is not accurate. In reality, there are always some losses, but they are minimized in streamlined flow. The use of the term "infinite" is not applicable to losses in streamlined flow.

options.text: Equal to what is in turbulent flow.
options.explanation: This option suggests that the loss of energy in streamlined flow is equal to that in turbulent flow. This is not true. Turbulent flow is characterized by chaotic and irregular movement of fluid particles, leading to higher frictional losses compared to the more ordered and smooth flow in streamlined conditions. Therefore, the losses in streamlined flow are generally less than those in turbulent flow.

questions.explanation: In the case of streamlined (laminar) flow of liquid, the loss of energy is typically at a minimum. This is because streamlined flow is characterized by smooth and orderly movement of fluid particles in parallel layers, resulting in lower frictional losses compared to turbulent flow.

questions.text: A car engine is based on the principle of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Bernoulli's equation is a fundamental principle in fluid dynamics, describing the conservation of energy in a flowing fluid. It relates the pressure, velocity, and elevation of points within a fluid flow. While Bernoulli's equation is important for understanding fluid dynamics, it is not directly related to the principles governing the operation of a car engine.

The Venturi effect describes the reduction in fluid pressure that occurs when a fluid flows through a constricted section of a pipe. The Venturi effect is utilized in devices like Venturi tubes and carburetors, but it is not the fundamental principle behind the operation of a car engine.

Torricelli's theorem is related to fluid dynamics and is often used to calculate the speed of efflux of fluid from a small opening in a container. While Torricelli's theorem is relevant to fluid flow, it is not the primary principle governing the functioning of a car engine.

The car engine operates based on the principles of thermodynamics and internal combustion, and it is not directly based on Bernoulli's equation, Venturi effect, or Torricelli's theorem. The combustion of fuel and air mixture in the engine cylinders generates power that drives the vehicle. This process involves concepts such as the Otto cycle (for spark-ignition engines) or the Diesel cycle (for compression-ignition engines), which are part of the broader field of thermodynamics.

questions.explanation: The car engine operates based on the principles of thermodynamics and internal combustion, and it is not directly based on Bernoulli's equation, Venturi effect, or Torricelli's theorem. The combustion of fuel and air mixture in the engine cylinders generates power that drives the vehicle. This process involves concepts such as the Otto cycle (for spark-ignition engines) or the Diesel cycle (for compression-ignition engines), which are part of the broader field of thermodynamics.

options.explanation: Torricelli's effect is related to the speed of efflux of a fluid from a small hole, not pressure

The Venturi effect involves the reduction in fluid pressure in a constricted portion of a pipe
Doppler's effect is related to the change in frequency of a wave due to relative motion

questions.explanation: Bernoulli's principle describes the decrease in pressure with an increase in fluid speed in a horizontal tube

questions.text: A two-meter-high tank is full of water. A hole is made in the middle of the tank. The speed of efflux is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: To find the speed of efflux from a hole in a container filled with water, Torricelli's theorem is used: v = \sqrt{2gh}, where g is the acceleration due to gravity 9.8m/s^2 and h is the depth of the water above the hole. Substituting h = 1m, we get v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.8 \times 1} = 4.9m/s.
This would be the speed if the hole were at the bottom of the tank, but the hole is in the middle, so the effective height is half of the tank's height.
This value does not accurately represent the speed of efflux from the hole.
This value is not consistent with the calculation based on Torricelli's theorem for the given conditions.

questions.explanation: To find the speed of efflux from a hole in a container filled with water, Torricelli's theorem is used: v = \sqrt{2gh}, where g is the acceleration due to gravity 9.8m/s^2 and h is the depth of the water above the hole. Substituting h = 1m, we get v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.8 \times 1} = 4.9m/s.

questions.text: What is the dimension of the coefficient of viscosity?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The coefficient of viscosity (η) is a measure of a fluid's resistance to flow. It is defined as the ratio of shear stress to shear strain rate.Shear stress (τ) is defined as the force per unit area acting parallel to the surface of the fluid. Its dimension is ML⁻¹T⁻².Shear strain rate (γ̇) is defined as the rate of change of the angle between two adjacent fluid layers. Its dimension is T⁻¹.Therefore, the dimension of the coefficient of viscosity (η) is:η = τ / γ̇ = (ML⁻¹T⁻²) / (T⁻¹) = ML⁻¹T⁻¹

questions.explanation: The coefficient of viscosity (η) is a measure of a fluid's resistance to flow. It is defined as the ratio of shear stress to shear strain rate.Shear stress (τ) is defined as the force per unit area acting parallel to the surface of the fluid. Its dimension is ML⁻¹T⁻².Shear strain rate (γ̇) is defined as the rate of change of the angle between two adjacent fluid layers. Its dimension is T⁻¹.Therefore, the dimension of the coefficient of viscosity (η) is:η = τ / γ̇ = (ML⁻¹T⁻²) / (T⁻¹) = ML⁻¹T⁻¹

questions.text: The question is: Blood pressure of a healthy person is _ mm Hg
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Blood pressure is measured in millimeters of mercury (mmHg) and is expressed as a ratio of systolic pressure (the pressure when the heart beats) over diastolic pressure (the pressure when the heart rests between beats). A healthy blood pressure reading is typically around 120/80 mmHg.

questions.explanation: Blood pressure is measured in millimeters of mercury (mmHg) and is expressed as a ratio of systolic pressure (the pressure when the heart beats) over diastolic pressure (the pressure when the heart rests between beats). A healthy blood pressure reading is typically around 120/80 mmHg.

questions.text: The question is: In a river flow of water is maximum at
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In a river flow, the water velocity is maximum at the middle due to less friction with the riverbed and banks. The water near the edges experiences more friction, slowing down the flow

questions.text: The question is: If an object sinks in the water, then
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: An object sinks in water if its density is greater than the density of water. Density is defined as mass per unit volume.If the object is denser than water, it means that it has more mass packed into the same volume compared to water. This causes the object to experience a greater downward force due to gravity (weight) than the upward buoyant force exerted by the water.

questions.explanation: An object sinks in water if its density is greater than the density of water. Density is defined as mass per unit volume.If the object is denser than water, it means that it has more mass packed into the same volume compared to water. This causes the object to experience a greater downward force due to gravity (weight) than the upward buoyant force exerted by the water.

questions.text: The question is: If the radii of two droplets are in the ratio 2:3, what will be the ratio of their Terminal velocities?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The terminal velocity of a spherical object falling through a viscous fluid is directly proportional to the square of its radius. This is given by the following equation:v = (2/9) * (ρ - ρ') * g * r^2 / ηwhere:v is the terminal velocityρ is the density of the objectρ' is the density of the fluidg is the acceleration due to gravityr is the radius of the objectη is the viscosity of the fluidSince the densities, acceleration due to gravity, and viscosity are constant for both droplets, the ratio of their terminal velocities is directly proportional to the square of the ratio of their radii.Therefore, if the radii of the two droplets are in the ratio 2:3, their terminal velocities will be in the ratio (2^2) : (3^2) = 4:9.

questions.explanation: The terminal velocity of a spherical object falling through a viscous fluid is directly proportional to the square of its radius. This is given by the following equation:v = (2/9) * (ρ - ρ') * g * r^2 / ηwhere:v is the terminal velocityρ is the density of the objectρ' is the density of the fluidg is the acceleration due to gravityr is the radius of the objectη is the viscosity of the fluidSince the densities, acceleration due to gravity, and viscosity are constant for both droplets, the ratio of their terminal velocities is directly proportional to the square of the ratio of their radii.Therefore, if the radii of the two droplets are in the ratio 2:3, their terminal velocities will be in the ratio (2^2) : (3^2) = 4:9.
